title: AI Company Security Incident Ledger
description: AI company security incidents from 2023 to 2026 with disclosure dates, classifications, scopes, CVEs, and primary sources. Covers confirmed breaches, researcher-disclosed exposures, vulnerability disclosures, supply-chain incidents, and exploitation campaigns. Not security advice; confirm against the linked primary sources.
citation: Vorp Labs, AI Company Security Incident Ledger (as of 2026-07-10), https://vorplabs.com/ai-threat-monitoring/security-incidents

incidents.name: ChatGPT Redis cache bug

scope.summary: A Redis client library bug caused a small percentage of users to see another user's payment-related information and chat history titles. Approximately 1.2% of ChatGPT Plus subscribers who were active during a specific window were affected.

incidents.summary: OpenAI disclosed that a bug in the open-source Redis client library redis-py caused ChatGPT to show some users' chat titles and, for a smaller subset, payment-related information (first and last name, email, payment address, last four digits of a credit card, and expiration date) belonging to other active users during a nine-hour window on March 20, 2023.
incidents.vendorResponse: OpenAI took ChatGPT offline, patched the Redis library, and published a detailed incident report. They notified affected users.

factChecks.label: Disclosure
factChecks.value: OpenAI published an incident report on March 24, 2023, describing the Redis cache bug and the categories of data exposed.

factChecks.label: Scope
factChecks.value: OpenAI stated approximately 1.2% of ChatGPT Plus subscribers who were active during a specific window may have had payment information exposed to other users.

openItems: openai.com blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); URL verified via Internet Archive snapshot (2026-07-08).

incidents.name: API tokens exposed on GitHub

scope.summary: Lasso Security researchers found 1,681 valid HuggingFace API tokens exposed in public GitHub repositories and HuggingFace Spaces. Tokens belonged to organizations including Meta, Google, Microsoft, and VMware. Some were write-access tokens that could have allowed model poisoning.

incidents.summary: Lasso Security published research documenting 1,681 valid HuggingFace API tokens found in public GitHub repositories and HuggingFace Spaces. The exposed tokens included write-access credentials belonging to employees at Meta, Google, Microsoft, and VMware, among others. Write-access tokens could have allowed an attacker to modify models hosted on the HuggingFace Hub.
incidents.vendorResponse: HuggingFace acknowledged the findings and worked with Lasso Security to revoke exposed tokens.

factChecks.label: Token count
factChecks.value: Lasso Security documented 1,681 valid HuggingFace API tokens in public repositories, including tokens with write access to major organizations' model repositories.

incidents.name: ShadowRay exploitation campaign

scope.summary: Oligo Security documented active exploitation of CVE-2023-48022 across thousands of publicly exposed Ray servers used for AI workloads. Attackers accessed sensitive data including cloud credentials, models, and datasets, and hijacked compute for cryptocurrency mining; exploitation had been under way for roughly seven months before disclosure.

incidents.summary: Oligo Security published research showing that CVE-2023-48022, a disputed vulnerability in the Ray AI framework (Anyscale considered the lack of authentication to be by design rather than a vulnerability), was being actively exploited across thousands of publicly exposed Ray clusters. Attackers used the unauthenticated access to steal credentials, cloud tokens, and production data, and to deploy cryptocurrency miners.
incidents.vendorResponse: Anyscale maintained that Ray is designed to run in trusted environments and that the lack of built-in authentication is documented behavior, not a vulnerability. They published guidance on securing Ray deployments.

factChecks.label: CVE status
factChecks.value: CVE-2023-48022 is classified as a disputed vulnerability. Anyscale considers the behavior by design; NIST NVD lists it as a critical severity issue (CVSS 9.8).

factChecks.label: Exploitation scope
factChecks.value: Oligo Security documented active exploitation across thousands of Ray clusters, with attackers deploying cryptominers and exfiltrating credentials and cloud tokens.

incidents.name: Cross-tenant model access vulnerability

scope.summary: Wiz researchers demonstrated that a malicious model running on Replicate's platform could escape its container and access other customers' AI models, prompts, and training data through cross-tenant isolation flaws.

incidents.summary: Wiz Research disclosed a cross-tenant vulnerability in Replicate's AI-as-a-service platform. By running a specially crafted model, a researcher could escape the container sandbox and access other tenants' models, prompts, and training data. Replicate patched the vulnerability within days of responsible disclosure.
incidents.vendorResponse: Replicate acknowledged the vulnerability, patched it within days, and published a security update.

factChecks.label: Vulnerability
factChecks.value: Wiz researchers demonstrated cross-tenant access through container escape on Replicate's shared AI infrastructure.

incidents.name: Spaces secrets unauthorized access

scope.summary: HuggingFace detected unauthorized access to its Spaces platform, specifically related to Spaces secrets. A subset of secrets may have been accessed without authorization. HuggingFace revoked affected tokens, engaged forensic specialists, and reported to law enforcement and data protection authorities.

incidents.summary: HuggingFace disclosed that it detected unauthorized access to its Spaces platform involving Spaces secrets (API tokens and keys stored by users); a subset may have been accessed. The company revoked affected HuggingFace tokens, engaged external cybersecurity forensic specialists, implemented a key management service for Spaces secrets, and reported the incident to law enforcement and data protection authorities.
incidents.vendorResponse: HuggingFace revoked compromised tokens, deprecated classic read/write tokens in favor of fine-grained access tokens, implemented KMS for Spaces secrets, and engaged external forensic specialists.

factChecks.label: Disclosure
factChecks.value: HuggingFace published a blog post on May 31, 2024, disclosing unauthorized access to Spaces secrets and describing remediation steps including token revocation and forensic engagement.

factChecks.label: Remediation
factChecks.value: HuggingFace revoked affected tokens, implemented KMS for Spaces secrets, deprecated classic tokens, and reported to law enforcement and data protection authorities.

incidents.name: Probllama path traversal and remote code execution

scope.summary: CVE-2024-37032 allowed path traversal leading to arbitrary file write and remote code execution on Ollama instances. The vulnerability was particularly severe in Docker installations where the server runs as root and listens on 0.0.0.0 by default.

incidents.summary: Wiz Research disclosed CVE-2024-37032 (nicknamed Probllama), a path traversal vulnerability in Ollama that allowed arbitrary file write and remote code execution. The digest field in manifest files lacked proper validation, enabling an attacker to write files anywhere on the filesystem. In Docker installations, Ollama runs as root and listens on 0.0.0.0, making remote exploitation straightforward.
incidents.vendorResponse: Ollama committed a fix approximately four hours after receiving the initial report on May 5, 2024, and released patched version 0.1.34 on May 8, 2024.

factChecks.label: CVE and fix
factChecks.value: CVE-2024-37032 was reported to Ollama on May 5, 2024, fixed the same day, and released in version 0.1.34 on May 8, 2024. Wiz published the research on June 24, 2024.

incidents.name: R1 leaked internal code and SaaS API keys

scope.summary: Internal source code containing working SaaS API keys, leaked by an employee to a third-party group. Rabbit names the ElevenLabs key (access to bulk pseudo-anonymized text-to-speech data and global r1 voice settings) and the SendGrid key (customer-facing email sending), and states no customer data was exposed.

incidents.summary: A Rabbit employee leaked internal source code containing working SaaS API keys to a third-party group, following earlier hardcoded-key findings published by the Rabbitude research collective. Rabbit's investigation names the ElevenLabs and SendGrid keys and reports the only observed abuse of the keys was sending defamatory emails.
incidents.vendorResponse: Rabbit rotated and revoked the exposed secrets, migrated keys into AWS Secrets Manager, terminated the responsible employee, disabled ElevenLabs history logging, commissioned a third-party codebase audit, and added automated checks against committing secrets.

factChecks.label: Vendor investigation
factChecks.value: Rabbit's security investigation updates (June 26 through July 5, 2024) state that leaked internal code included working API keys, name the ElevenLabs and SendGrid keys, and report that no customer data was exposed and the only observed abuse was defamatory emails.

openItems: Rabbitude's original publication (rabbitu.de) was unreachable (HTTP 500) as of 2026-07-10; scope claims beyond Rabbit's own investigation are not independently linked.

incidents.name: SAPwned: AI Core cross-tenant vulnerabilities

scope.summary: Wiz researchers found multiple vulnerabilities in SAP AI Core that allowed cross-tenant access to other customers' AI models, training data, and cloud credentials (AWS, Azure, SAP HANA). The vulnerabilities chained together to achieve full tenant isolation bypass.

incidents.summary: Wiz Research disclosed a set of vulnerabilities in SAP AI Core, collectively named SAPwned, that could have let an attacker escape their tenant and access other customers' data, AI models, and cloud credentials. The demonstrated chain reached Kubernetes cluster access, AWS tokens, Azure credentials, and SAP HANA database credentials belonging to other tenants. No evidence of exploitation in the wild was reported at disclosure.
incidents.vendorResponse: SAP fixed all reported vulnerabilities. No evidence of exploitation in the wild was found at the time of disclosure.

factChecks.label: Vulnerability scope
factChecks.value: Wiz documented cross-tenant access to AI models, training data, and cloud credentials (AWS, Azure, SAP HANA) through SAP AI Core isolation bypass.

incidents.name: Copilot Studio server-side request forgery

scope.summary: CVE-2024-38206 could let an attacker bypass SSRF protections in Microsoft Copilot Studio, access instance metadata and managed identity tokens, and reach internal resources including Cosmos DB instances.

incidents.summary: Tenable disclosed CVE-2024-38206, a server-side request forgery vulnerability in Microsoft Copilot Studio (on the Power Platform). An attacker could circumvent SSRF protections to access instance metadata, managed identity tokens, and internal resources including Cosmos DB. Microsoft rated the vulnerability as critical severity.
incidents.vendorResponse: Microsoft pushed fixes to all affected services and regions by late July 2024. No customer action was required. The CVE was published in August 2024.

factChecks.label: CVE and severity
factChecks.value: CVE-2024-38206 was rated critical by Microsoft. Tenable disclosed the vulnerability on June 21, 2024; Microsoft fixed it by late July 2024.

factChecks.label: Fix status
factChecks.value: Microsoft pushed fixes to all affected services and regions. No customer action required.

incidents.name: Container Toolkit container escape

scope.summary: CVE-2024-0132 could let an attacker controlling a container image escape the container and gain full access to the underlying host system. All versions of NVIDIA Container Toolkit up to v1.16.1 and GPU Operator up to 24.6.1 were affected.

incidents.summary: Wiz Research disclosed CVE-2024-0132, a critical container escape vulnerability in NVIDIA Container Toolkit and GPU Operator. An attacker with control over a container image could escape the container and gain full access to the underlying host system, including the host filesystem. The vulnerability exploited the fact that Unix sockets remain writable even when mounted with read-only permissions.
incidents.vendorResponse: NVIDIA released patched versions (Container Toolkit v1.16.2 and GPU Operator v24.6.2) on September 26, 2024, the same day the vulnerability was publicly disclosed.

factChecks.label: CVE and scope
factChecks.value: CVE-2024-0132 affected all NVIDIA Container Toolkit versions up to v1.16.1 and GPU Operator up to 24.6.1. Wiz reported it on September 1, 2024; NVIDIA acknowledged September 3 and patched September 26.

incidents.name: Platform data breach

scope.summary: A threat actor claimed to have breached Muah.AI, an AI companion chatbot platform, and the breach was verified by Have I Been Pwned. The exposed dataset contained 1.9 million user accounts.

incidents.summary: A threat actor claimed to have breached Muah.AI, an AI companion chatbot platform, in September 2024. Have I Been Pwned independently verified the breach data and added it on October 8, 2024, listing 1.9 million compromised accounts.

factChecks.label: HIBP verification
factChecks.value: Have I Been Pwned added the Muah.AI breach on October 8, 2024, listing 1.9 million compromised accounts with a breach date of September 2024.

openItems: Specific data classes compromised not confirmed from the HIBP listing page; full breach details in the HIBP individual breach record.

incidents.name: PyPI package compromise with cryptominer

scope.summary: Versions 8.3.41, 8.3.42, 8.3.45, and 8.3.46 of the Ultralytics Python package on PyPI were affected and removed. The initial compromise injected an XMRig cryptocurrency miner; the PyPI wheel differed from the corresponding GitHub source, indicating the publishing pipeline was the attack vector rather than the source repository.

incidents.summary: The Ultralytics YOLO computer vision library (a popular open-source AI package) had its PyPI publishing pipeline compromised, resulting in version 8.3.41 containing an XMRig cryptocurrency miner injected into utils/downloads.py. The malicious code was present only in the PyPI-published wheel, not in the GitHub source repository at the same tag, confirming a pipeline attack rather than a source code compromise.
incidents.vendorResponse: Ultralytics removed the compromised version from PyPI and published a fix via pull request #18111 on GitHub.

factChecks.label: Compromise details
factChecks.value: GitHub issue #18027, opened December 5, 2024, documents that Ultralytics v8.3.41 on PyPI contained a modified utils/downloads.py file invoking an XMRig cryptocurrency miner. The PyPI wheel did not match the GitHub source for the same tag.

incidents.name: Exposed ClickHouse database with user data

scope.summary: Wiz Research discovered a publicly accessible, completely unauthenticated ClickHouse database belonging to DeepSeek containing over one million lines of log entries with plaintext chat histories, API secret keys, and backend operational details. Logs dated from January 6, 2025.

incidents.summary: Wiz Research discovered a publicly accessible ClickHouse database belonging to DeepSeek at oauth2callback.deepseek.com and dev.deepseek.com. The database required no authentication and contained over one million log entries including plaintext user chat histories, API secret keys, backend operational details, and internal API endpoint references. Wiz responsibly disclosed the issue and DeepSeek promptly secured the exposure.
incidents.vendorResponse: DeepSeek promptly secured the exposure after responsible disclosure by Wiz Research.

factChecks.label: Exposure details
factChecks.value: Wiz documented a completely open, unauthenticated ClickHouse database at oauth2callback.deepseek.com containing over one million log entries with plaintext chat histories, API keys, and backend details.

factChecks.label: Data not extracted
factChecks.value: Wiz stated they limited themselves to enumeration and did not run intrusive queries, preserving ethical research practices.

incidents.name: Drift OAuth-token theft and downstream Salesforce data exfiltration

scope.summary: A threat actor stole OAuth tokens for the Drift-Salesforce integration and used them between August 8 and August 18, 2025 to exfiltrate data from customers' Salesforce instances. FINRA characterized the incident as a supply-chain attack impacting more than 700 organizations. Salesloft's investigation found the attacker had accessed Salesloft's GitHub from March through June 2025 and reached Drift's AWS environment.

incidents.summary: Salesloft disclosed that a threat actor used OAuth credentials for its Drift AI chat-agent integration to exfiltrate data from customers' Salesforce instances between August 8 and August 18, 2025. Salesforce disabled the Drift and Salesloft integrations in response, and Salesloft's investigation with Mandiant traced the intrusion back to access to Salesloft's GitHub beginning in March 2025.
incidents.vendorResponse: Salesloft published trust-center updates, engaged Mandiant, rotated credentials, and worked with Salesforce, which disabled the Drift integrations while the incident was remediated.

factChecks.label: Mechanism and window
factChecks.value: Salesloft's trust update states that from August 8 to August 18, 2025, a threat actor used OAuth credentials to exfiltrate data from customers' Salesforce instances.

factChecks.label: Salesforce response
factChecks.value: Salesforce disabled integrations between Salesforce and Salesloft technologies, including Drift, after identifying the incident.

factChecks.label: Impact count
factChecks.value: FINRA described the incident as a supply-chain attack through the Drift chatbot integration impacting more than 700 organizations.

openItems: finra.org blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); alert content verified via rendered-page check on 2026-07-10.
Per-customer records exfiltrated were not quantified by Salesloft; downstream customer notifications are tracked by the affected organizations.

incidents.name: Mixpanel analytics-vendor breach affecting OpenAI API users

scope.summary: An attacker gained unauthorized access to part of Mixpanel's systems and exported a dataset containing limited customer-identifiable analytics data from the frontend of OpenAI's API product: name, email address, coarse location, operating system and browser, referring websites, and OpenAI organization or user IDs.

incidents.summary: OpenAI disclosed that Mixpanel, a third-party analytics provider used on the frontend of its API product, detected an attacker who had gained unauthorized access to part of Mixpanel's systems on November 9, 2025 and exported a dataset that included limited identifiable information about OpenAI API users. The incident occurred in Mixpanel's systems, not OpenAI's.
incidents.vendorResponse: OpenAI removed Mixpanel from production, reviewed the affected dataset Mixpanel shared on November 25, notified affected users and organizations, and terminated its use of Mixpanel.

factChecks.label: Disclosure
factChecks.value: OpenAI's disclosure states Mixpanel detected unauthorized access on November 9, 2025, and shared the affected dataset with OpenAI on November 25, 2025; exposed fields include name, email, coarse location, browser and OS, referrers, and org or user IDs.

factChecks.label: Scope limitation
factChecks.value: OpenAI stated that chats, prompts, API requests, API usage data, passwords, credentials, API keys, payment details, and government IDs were not exposed; no passwords or OpenAI systems were involved.

openItems: openai.com blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); disclosure verified via Internet Archive snapshot (2026-06-04).
Exact number of affected users not publicly disclosed.

incidents.name: ShadowRay 2.0 continued exploitation campaign

scope.summary: Oligo Security documented a second wave of exploitation targeting Ray clusters, finding continued active compromise of AI infrastructure using the same CVE-2023-48022 attack vector with evolved techniques.

incidents.summary: Oligo Security published follow-up research documenting continued exploitation of Ray clusters using CVE-2023-48022, the same vulnerability from the original ShadowRay campaign. The second wave featured evolved attack techniques and demonstrated that many Ray deployments remained unprotected despite the original disclosure in March 2024.

factChecks.label: Continued exploitation
factChecks.value: Oligo Security documented a second wave of CVE-2023-48022 exploitation in November 2025, showing that many Ray clusters remained publicly exposed without authentication.

incidents.name: Data breach (California AG filing)

scope.summary: Mercor.io Corporation filed a data breach notice with the California Attorney General reporting incidents on March 24 and March 30, 2026. The filing is listed under breach report SB24-625431. The consumer notice PDF contains breach details.

incidents.summary: Mercor, an AI-powered hiring and talent marketplace platform, filed data breach notice SB24-625431 with the California Attorney General, disclosed June 25, 2026. The filing confirms breach dates of March 24 and March 30, 2026. Specific data elements and the number of affected Californians are detailed in the linked consumer notice PDF.

factChecks.label: AG filing
factChecks.value: California Attorney General data breach filing SB24-625431 lists Mercor.io Corporation with breach dates of March 24, 2026, and March 30, 2026.

openItems: Consumer notice PDF not fetched by automated tools; specific data elements and affected count need manual review of the PDF.

incidents.name: Malicious Axios version in macOS app-signing workflow

scope.summary: A GitHub Actions workflow used in OpenAI's macOS app-signing process downloaded and executed a malicious version of the Axios library (1.14.1) during a broader npm supply-chain attack. The workflow had access to code-signing and notarization material; OpenAI found no evidence that user data, systems, intellectual property, or shipped software were compromised or altered.

incidents.summary: OpenAI disclosed that on March 31, 2026 (UTC), a GitHub Actions workflow in its macOS app-signing pipeline executed malicious Axios version 1.14.1 as part of a broader software supply-chain attack. Out of caution, OpenAI treated its macOS code-signing certificate as compromised even though its analysis found no evidence of misuse.
incidents.vendorResponse: OpenAI rotated the macOS signing certificate, released newly signed builds, and required users to update the macOS app by May 8, 2026.

factChecks.label: Disclosure
factChecks.value: OpenAI's disclosure states the malicious Axios 1.14.1 executed in a GitHub Actions macOS app-signing workflow on 2026-03-31, that no evidence of compromised user data, systems, IP, or software was found, and that the signing certificate was rotated with app updates required by 2026-05-08.

openItems: openai.com blocks automated clients (HTTP 403); disclosure verified via Internet Archive snapshot (2026-06-25).

incidents.name: SDK supply-chain compromise

scope.summary: A compromised version of a Mistral SDK package was published, containing unauthorized modifications. Mistral identified the compromise and published a security advisory with affected version details.

incidents.summary: Mistral disclosed that a version of one of its SDK packages was compromised and published with unauthorized modifications. The company identified the compromise, pulled the affected versions, and published a security advisory directing users to upgrade to a clean version.
incidents.vendorResponse: Mistral pulled the affected package versions and published a security advisory with upgrade instructions.

factChecks.label: Advisory
factChecks.value: Mistral published a security advisory in May 2026 detailing the SDK package compromise and affected versions.

openItems: Exact affected version range needs confirmation from Mistral's security advisory page.
Mistral security advisories page URL needs manual verification.

excludedReports.name: Internal forum unauthorized access (2023, media reports)
excludedReports.reason: Media outlets including The New York Times reported that an attacker accessed OpenAI's internal messaging forum in early 2023. OpenAI has not published a disclosure or incident report, and no attorney general filing is known. This ledger requires a vendor disclosure, regulator filing, or original researcher writeup as the primary source.

excludedReports.name: Google Docs data exposure (media reports)
excludedReports.reason: Media outlets reported that Scale AI exposed sensitive data through improperly shared Google Docs. Scale AI has not published a disclosure, and no attorney general filing is known. This ledger requires a primary source beyond media reporting.

excludedReports.name: December 2024 teen safety incident
excludedReports.reason: The December 2024 Character.AI incident involved user safety and platform moderation concerns (a minor's death linked to chatbot interactions), not unauthorized access to data systems or a cybersecurity breach. Character.AI's response focused on safety guardrails, not data security. This ledger covers cybersecurity incidents, not platform safety events.

excludedReports.name: MCP integration security concern (June 2025, no official notice)
excludedReports.reason: Security researchers flagged potential data exposure through Asana's MCP (Model Context Protocol) integration. No official vendor notice, security advisory, or data breach filing from Asana was found on asana.com, the Asana community forum, or state AG breach databases as of this review.

excludedReports.name: Alleged March 2026 PyPI supply-chain compromise
excludedReports.reason: A PyPI supply-chain compromise of LiteLLM was reported in some secondary sources. Review of BerriAI/litellm GitHub security advisories found multiple code-level vulnerabilities (SQL injection, SSTI, MCP auth bypass, etc.) in April–June 2026, but no supply-chain incident involving a compromised PyPI package. This ledger includes LiteLLM only if a supply-chain compromise can be verified via a PyPI advisory, GitHub advisory, or maintainer disclosure.

excludedReports.name: Platform breach (media reports only)
excludedReports.reason: Media outlets reported a breach of OmniGPT. No vendor disclosure, AG filing, or original researcher writeup was found. This ledger requires a primary source.

changeLog.label: Ledger launch
changeLog.change: Initial publication with 19 incidents from 2023 through 2026 and 6 excluded reports.

changeLog.label: Mercor CA AG filing
changeLog.change: Mercor filed a data breach notice with the California Attorney General (SB24-625431) reporting breach dates of March 24 and March 30, 2026.

changeLog.label: Mistral SDK
changeLog.change: Mistral disclosed a supply-chain compromise affecting its SDK package.

changeLog.label: OpenAI Axios
changeLog.change: OpenAI disclosed that a malicious Axios version executed in its macOS app-signing workflow; the signing certificate was rotated out of caution.

monitoringSources.note: Maine AG site has intermittent availability; checked when accessible.

monitoringSources.note: Most AI companies are private; relevant 8-K filings are rare.
